blurb: The Ptarmigan Traverse is a roughly 30-mile, off-trail alpine route along the North Cascades crest in Washington, running from Cascade Pass (North Cascades National Park) south to Dome Peak and out via Downey Creek (Glacier Peak Wilderness). It crosses five major glaciers (Cache, Middle Cascade, South Cascade, Le Conte, and Dana) and threads granodiorite/gneiss peaks including Formidable, Sentinel, Old Guard, and Spire Point, typically taking 4-8 days. First completed in July 1938 by four members of the Ptarmigan Climbing Club (Bill Cox, Calder Bressler, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers) over 13 days, it went unrepeated for 15 years before an 1953 reversal popularized it, and it remains one of the defining classic mountaineering routes of the range.
hierarchyNote: This entry is not a single summit: "Alpine and Technical Traverses" mirrors a Mountain Project grouping area (MP area id 116969335) for multi-day North Cascades traverse routes, and "Ptarmigan Traverse" (MP route id 116969398) is correctly filed under it — the "North Cascades" parent placement is geographically valid. However, the supplied coordinate (48.64649, -121.20827) is only a generic area-level pin: it matches Mountain Project's own coordinate for that area page and sits close to North Cascades National Park's general park-level coordinate (~48.71, -121.21), not any specific point on the actual ~30-mile route corridor, which runs from Cascade Pass (48.4683, -121.0600, 5,392 ft) south to Dome Peak (48.3034, -121.0295, 8,920+ ft) / the Downey Creek exit. No single elevationFt applies since this isn't one mountain.

routes.fa: Bill Cox, Calder Bressler, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers (Ptarmigan Climbing Club) — first full traverse, July 1938, 13 days
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 scrambling with occasional optional low-5th-class variations; no sustained technical rock climbing on the standard line
objHaz: Remoteness — no cell service, long self-rescue
routes.overview: A remote, off-trail alpine traverse along the North Cascades crest from Cascade Pass to Dome Peak/Downey Creek, linking five glaciers, alpine lake basins, and a few exposed rock steps rather than following any single summit line.
routes.beta: The Ptarmigan links Cascade Pass to the Suiattle River drainage by staying high along the crest, crossing Cache Col, dropping to Kool-Aid Lake, traversing exposed 'red ledges' below Old Guard/Sentinel, crossing the Spider-Formidable col, then working south across the Middle Cascade and South Cascade Glaciers to Yang-Yang and White Rock Lakes, over Le Conte/Dana Glacier terrain past Dome Peak (optional summit) and Sinister Peak, and finally down Itswoot Ridge to Cub Lake and out the Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek Trail. Most parties go north to south and take 4-8 days; a 2025 solo single push has been done in under 17 hours.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road east from Marblemount (paved ~10 miles, then gravel, then a narrow high-clearance shelf road past Eldorado at mile 20) to its end at mile 23.1, the Cascade Pass Trailhead. Hike the maintained Cascade Pass Trail roughly 3.7 miles and ~1,800 ft to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft), which is the last maintained trail and the start of the off-trail traverse.
routes.descent: Exit south via Itswoot Ridge and the Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek Trail to the Downey Creek Trailhead on the Suiattle River Road (FSR 26).
routes.descentText: From White Rock Lakes/Dome Peak, the route continues south across the Dana/Chickamin Glacier basin, over or around Dome Peak (optional class 3-4 summit) and Sinister Peak, then drops onto Itswoot Ridge. From Itswoot Ridge, a climbers' path descends steeply (~500+ ft to Cub Lake) and continues down through forest on the Bachelor Creek use-trail to its junction with the maintained Downey Creek Trail #768, following Downey Creek roughly 4-5 miles out to the Downey Creek Trailhead on the Suiattle River Road. As of 2026, this trailhead is only reachable by foot or bike beyond a washout closure a few miles up FSR 26, adding significant road-walking mileage to the standard exit.
routes.itinerary: Standard 5-7 day plan (per multiple trip reports): Day 1 - Cascade Pass Trailhead to Kool-Aid Lake (via Cache Col). Day 2 - Kool-Aid Lake to Yang-Yang Lakes (via the red ledges and Spider-Formidable col). Day 3 - Yang-Yang Lakes to White Rock Lakes (South Cascade Glacier). Day 4 - optional rest/weather day at White Rock Lakes. Day 5 - White Rock Lakes to Itswoot Ridge (Le Conte/Dana Glaciers, Dome Peak environs). Day 6 - Itswoot Ridge out to the Downey Creek Trailhead via Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek Trail. Fit parties compress this to 4 days; an unsupported single push has been done in under 17 hours.
routes.bail: In the first day or two (through Cache Col/Kool-Aid Lake), the simplest retreat is back over Cache Col to Cascade Pass and out the Cascade River Road trailhead. Beyond the Spider-Formidable col there is no easy short bail — parties generally must either continue south to the Downey Creek exit or reverse the glacier and col sections already crossed; a historical alternate has been descending old mining-road remnants near the South Cascade Glacier research station toward the Downey Creek drainage.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux terrain is multi-day glacier travel and exposed traversing rather than a single summit push, the key safety discipline is committing to a multi-day weather window before starting and being willing to hole up or reverse a day's progress on the glaciers/cols in poor visibility or lightning risk, rather than push on to a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early September is the reliable window: Cascade River Road is open, the worst early-season snow/avalanche hazard has passed, and the glaciers are consolidated enough for efficient travel, though bergschrunds and moats widen as the season progresses. Heavy-snow years can push a safe start later into July; by mid-to-late September the glaciers are at their iciest/most crevassed and the access road may face early storm closures.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service anywhere along the route, which lies deep in North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness; a satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) plus a filed itinerary and backcountry permit are the main safety net.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier/alpine rack rather than a rock rack: one rope per rope team (30m per 2 climbers, or a 60m single shared by 3), 1+ snow picket per 3 people (more early season), 2+ ice screws per rope team (seasonally dependent), full crevasse-rescue kits (harness, prusiks/mechanical ascenders, locking biners, pulley) for every person, plus a small token rock rack (nuts and a few small-to-mid cams) for the occasional exposed step.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are glacier-travel-oriented rather than rock-climbing-oriented: a crevasse-rescue kit and a shared rope per rope team are the priority, with pickets/screws sized to conditions (more needed in early/high-snow years, less by late summer); rock protection is a minor, optional addition.
gear: Glacier rope (30m or 60m, shared per rope team)
Small rack: nuts + a few cams
whatToBring: 4-8 days of food
Bear canister (may be required/loanable via NPS)
Lightweight backpacking tent/bivy
Water treatment
Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide (Stevens Pass to Rainy Pass) or equivalent route notes
GPS with a downloaded track
Satellite communicator
Sun protection for glacier travel
Trekking poles
Camp shoes for stream crossings
proTips: Go north-to-south (Cascade Pass to Downey Creek) — it's the standard direction most route descriptions and beta assume.
Carry Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide (Stevens Pass to Rainy Pass) for route-finding; the traverse is unsigned and unmaintained beyond Cascade Pass.
Cross the glaciers early in the morning while the snow is firm; afternoon softening raises both crevasse-fall and rockfall risk.
Confirm Cascade River Road and Suiattle River Road/Downey Creek trailhead status with NPS/USFS before you go — both have had recent closures and washouts.
watchOut: Cache Col's steep snow/ice slope can be firm and exposed in early season; some parties place a hand line.
The exposed 'red ledges' traverse below Old Guard/Sentinel is narrow, exposed scrambling with real fall consequences.
Late season (Aug-Sept) can expose bare glacier ice and open crevasses/bergschrunds, complicating travel.
Whiteout conditions on the broad, featureless Middle Cascade and South Cascade Glaciers can make navigation very difficult without a GPS track or strong map/compass skills.
knownHazards: Crevasses on five glaciers (Cache, Middle Cascade, South Cascade, Le Conte, Dana) require roped travel and crevasse-rescue competence.
Bergschrunds/moats can be difficult or impassable at several cols in early season.
Loose rock and class 3-4 scrambling on sections like the Spider-Formidable col and Dome Peak's summit ridge add rockfall and fall risk.
The route is remote, off-trail, and multi-day with no cell coverage — an injury or storm can mean a long, technical self-rescue or evacuation.

pitchDetail.pitch: Cascade Pass TH to Cache Col
pitchDetail.notes: Last maintained trail ends at Cascade Pass; climb north/east toward Cache Col, often on snow into midsummer.
Descend from the col into the Kool-Aid Lake basin, the traverse's first common bivy.
Crosses the narrow, exposed 'red ledges' and the Spider-Formidable col; a crux navigation/exposure section.

pitchDetail.pitch: Yang-Yang Lakes to White Rock Lakes
pitchDetail.notes: Crosses the Middle Cascade and South Cascade Glaciers; crevasse hazard, whiteout risk on featureless snow.

pitchDetail.pitch: White Rock Lakes to Itswoot Ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Crosses Le Conte/Dana Glacier terrain past Dome Peak (optional class 3-4 summit) and Sinister Peak.

pitchDetail.pitch: Itswoot Ridge to Downey Creek TH
pitchDetail.notes: Steep descent to Cub Lake, then the Bachelor Creek use-trail and Downey Creek Trail #768 out to the road.

road.name: Cascade River Road (north access, from Marblemount) / Suiattle River Road FSR 26 (south exit, from Darrington)
road.status: Cascade River Road is open seasonally to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (road-end, mile 23.1). Suiattle River Road is closed to vehicles at mile 4 (closure order in effect through at least Jan 1, 2028) after Dec 2025 flood damage, so the Downey Creek/Suiattle trailheads that form the traverse's standard southern exit are currently reachable only on foot or bike.
road.seasonalGate: Cascade River Road is gated in winter and typically opens by late June, sometimes not until early July after heavy-snow years; it can close early after fall storm damage.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, Cascade River Road is paved for about 10 miles, then rough gravel to mile 20 (Eldorado), then a narrow, rutted, high-clearance-recommended shelf road to the mile-23.1 Cascade Pass Trailhead; trailers/RVs over 22 ft are prohibited past mile 18. On the south side, reaching Downey Creek Trailhead currently requires walking or biking several extra miles of closed road past the FSR 26 washout.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (avalanche); NWS Zone Area Forecast for West Slopes North Cascades and Passes (weather)
climate.typical: Cool nights and warm, often sunny days once the summer high-pressure pattern sets in; afternoon thunderstorm risk increases through August.
climate.spring: Deep, unconsolidated snowpack with hidden crevasses; Cascade River Road is typically still closed.
climate.summer: Primary season (Jul-Aug): firm morning snow/glacier travel that softens by afternoon; occasional thunderstorms and wildfire smoke by late summer.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and possible first snow by late Sept/Oct; Cascade River Road can close early after storms.
climate.winter: Not a winter route — heavy snowpack, high avalanche hazard, and closed access roads make it impractical and unsafe.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS) on the northern half; Glacier Peak Wilderness within the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (USFS) on the southern half
access.fees: $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee for the NPS wilderness permit; a Northwest Forest Pass or interagency pass is required for parking at the USFS Downey Creek trailhead
access.permit: An NPS backcountry/wilderness permit is required for all overnight camping in North Cascades National Park — reserve via Recreation.gov (permit ID 4675322), watch the mandatory safety video, and pick up the permit by email or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at the Downey Creek trailhead (USFS); no day-use pass is required at the Cascade Pass trailhead inside the national park.
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FSR 26) closed to vehicles at mile 4 (through at least Jan 1, 2028) following Dec 2025 flood damage, blocking vehicle access to the Downey Creek/Suiattle trailheads that form the traverse's standard southern exit. Cascade River Road has routine winter closures and typically doesn't open to the Cascade Pass trailhead until late June.

timing.recommendedStart: Move early each day and cross glaciated/snow sections before afternoon sun softens them; commit to the route only after checking a multi-day forecast.

emergency.county: Skagit County (northern half, including Cascade Pass/Cache Col); Snohomish County (southern half, including Downey Creek/Suiattle drainage, Darrington Ranger District)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Call 911 for emergencies; Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (360) 416-1911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200; Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest — (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Remote, multi-day, off-trail traverse with no cell service. Rescue coordination for the NPS (northern) half runs through North Cascades National Park; for the Glacier Peak Wilderness/Downey Creek (southern) half it runs through the Snohomish County Sheriff and the Darrington Ranger District. A satellite communicator and a filed permit/itinerary are the primary way to trigger help.

routes.corrections: The given area-level coordinate (48.64649, -121.20827) is a generic North Cascades pin (matches Mountain Project's own coordinate for the 'Alpine and Technical Traverses' area, and sits near NOCA's general park coordinate) rather than a point on the actual route, which runs from Cascade Pass (48.4683, -121.0600) to Dome Peak (48.3034, -121.0295)/Downey Creek — see hierarchyNote. Mountain Project's route page also lists a technical 'Grade II' for this route, which conflicts with independent sources describing a 4-8 day, multi-glacier commitment; no reliable authoritative commitment grade (I-VI) could be sourced, so 'commitment' is left null rather than using the likely-erroneous MP value. Distance is reported inconsistently across sources (roughly 30-35+ miles); gainFt/lossFt (11,000 ft each way) and highPointFt (8,264 ft, Spire Point col on the standard route) follow the most detailed sourced trip-report data (stephabegg.com), noting Dome Peak's optional summit is higher (8,920+ ft).

blurb: Mount Terror (8,151 ft) is the highest summit of the Southern Pickets, a subrange of the Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, about 16 miles south of the Canadian border. Built of glacially-sculpted gneiss and granite, it is one of the range's most remote and committing objectives, with a gentler south/west aspect rising ~1,000 ft from Crescent Creek Basin and a dramatic 2,500-ft north face dropping into the McMillan Creek cirque. The peak was first climbed via its south side in 1932 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg of the Seattle Mountaineers; the steep north face was not climbed until 1961.

routes.fa: Original North Face: Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, Dave Hiser, Mike Swayne, July 8-11, 1961 (III 5.7, right of the buttress crest). Harder buttress-crest variant ('Stoddard Buttress'): John Stoddard, solo, July 16, 1984 (IV 5.8).
routes.rappels: Descend via the West Ridge to the Blob-Terror notch (rappel off fixed but reportedly 'sketchy' pitons), then down the gully to Crescent Creek Cirque; number of rappels depends on snow/rock conditions. The original 1961 party descended via four steep rappels down the east corner when off-route.
objHaz: rockfall / loose rock (documented serious fall accident)
crevasses crossing the Mustard Glacier approach
bergschrund/moat at the base of the face
routes.season: Jul-Sep; early season (Jul) favors an easier, snow-covered glacier crossing to the base
routes.overview: A serious alpine rock line up the imposing 2,500-ft north side of Mount Terror above the McMillan Creek/Mustard Glacier cirque. The original 1961 route takes the line of least resistance to the right of the buttress crest; the harder, more sustained 'Stoddard Buttress' (1984) follows the crest itself in bold style, finishing on an exposed 5.8+ knife-edge ridge to a false summit.
routes.beta: From Crescent Creek Basin, cross the Ottohorn-Himmelhorn col and traverse the Mustard Glacier (easiest early-season) to reach 3rd/4th-class scrambling that gains the snow patch below the face, where roping up begins. The Stoddard line starts at the toe of the buttress, traverses ledges left, then climbs solid rock straight up the crest through sustained 5.7-5.8 terrain (much of it simulclimbable) to a false summit and a blocky 5.8+ knife-edge ridge. An alternative, more direct but brushier approach follows Stetattle Ridge into the upper McMillan Creek cirque.
routes.approach: Approach via Goodell Creek trailhead (across SR-20 from Newhalem) to Terror Basin/Crescent Creek Basin (multi-day, off-trail, ~19 mi round trip to the peak's south side). From a high camp in Crescent Creek Basin, cross the Ottohorn-Himmelhorn col and traverse the Mustard Glacier to the base of the north face; even fast parties need roughly 2-3 hours from Crescent Creek camp to the base of the route. An alternate approach reaches the McMillan Creek cirque directly via Stetattle Ridge - described as brush-free on the way in but with a reportedly brutal, six-hour bushwhack exit back to the Goodell Creek trail.
routes.descent: Down the West Ridge to the Blob-Terror notch, then rappel/downclimb the gully into Crescent Creek Cirque.
routes.descentText: From the summit, scramble/downclimb the West Ridge to the Blob-Terror notch. A rappel (historically off worn/sketchy fixed pitons) drops into the gully leading back to Crescent Creek Cirque; multiple rappels may be needed depending on snow cover. In early season this can be a quick ~20-minute descent to camp; late season with more exposed rock it is slower and looser.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike/bushwhack from Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin or Crescent Creek Basin camp (5-8 hrs). Day 2: cross to a high camp near the Otto-Himmel col or upper McMillan cirque. Day 3: climb the north face/buttress and descend to camp. Day 4-5: exit via Goodell Creek trail.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once well established on the upper buttress. The safest bail is to reverse the route below the midway ledges before committing to the crest; the 1961 first-ascent party bivouacked mid-route and down-climbed via steep rappels off the east corner after getting off-route.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, off-trail approach and lack of any quick escape from the upper face, set a firm turnaround time before starting the technical climbing and honor it - being caught overnight unprepared on the buttress, or crossing the glacier approach in deteriorating visibility, are the principal risks.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through early September, once the Mustard Glacier approach has consolidated snow bridges but before late-season melt-out exposes crevasses and loosens rock on the upper buttress; late August into September brings drier rock but shortening days and increasing fall-snow risk.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Southern Pickets backcountry; this is remote, multi-day terrain far from any maintained trail, so a satellite messenger/PLB is strongly advised.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack (small-to-large cams, set of stoppers), extra slings for the knife-edge sections, plus full glacier-travel gear (rope, crevasse-rescue kit, axe and crampons) for the Mustard Glacier crossing on approach.
routes.proNeeds: Alpine rock rack plus glacier gear; described by one source simply as needing 'alpine rock rack, glacier gear, perhaps a machete' for the brushy approach options.
gear: glacier travel gear (axe, crampons, crevasse-rescue kit)
whatToBring: multi-day bivy/camp gear
extra fuel and water capacity
satellite communicator/PLB
loppers or willingness to bushwhack on alternate approaches
proTips: Cross the Mustard Glacier early in the season while snow bridges are intact - a late-season crossing is notably harder and more exposed to crevasse hazard.
The 'Stoddard Buttress' crest line (1984 solo FA) is the cleaner, more popular modern objective; the original 1961 line to its right is easier (5.7) but less aesthetic.
The approach is frequently harder than the climbing itself - budget real time and energy for it.
watchOut: Rockfall/loose rock on the buttress has caused at least one serious documented fall accident - wear a helmet and move carefully through simulclimbed terrain.
The Stetattle Ridge approach variant has a reportedly brutal bushwhacking exit if you can't retrace your inbound line.
knownHazards: Loose, sometimes large detached blocks on the buttress that can fail underfoot (documented AAC incident).
Crevasse hazard crossing the Mustard Glacier, more pronounced later in the season.
Difficult, brush-choked terrain on the Stetattle Ridge/McMillan Creek approach alternative.

pitchDetail.notes: Ledge traverse left of the toe of the buttress, then onto solid rock on the ridge crest
Sustained, largely simulclimbable rock up the crest
Blocky, exposed ridge crest to a false summit (Stoddard variant)
Line of least resistance right of the buttress crest; steep névé and firm weathered granite

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Goodell Creek Road
road.status: SR-20 is open year-round at this western stretch near Newhalem
road.seasonalGate: None at this location - SR-20's seasonal winter closure gate is well east of here, near Diablo/Washington Pass, and does not affect access to Newhalem/Goodell Creek
road.driveNote: From Newhalem, cross Goodell Creek and take the unpaved Goodell Creek Road spur (across SR-20 from the Newhalem campground) to the trailhead/parking area.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry / Washington Pass region
climate.typical: Wet, snowy marine-influenced alpine climate with heavy winter snowpack and a short, generally reliable summer climbing window
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June/July; approach trails and cols often still snow-covered
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Aug); afternoon thunderstorms possible, otherwise the most settled weather of the year
climate.fall: Climbable into September with care for early-season snow and rapidly shortening daylight
climate.winter: Effectively inaccessible for technical climbing due to heavy snow and avalanche hazard

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No climbing fee; a backcountry/wilderness permit is required for overnight stays
access.permit: Backcountry permit for the relevant cross-country zone (e.g. Crescent Creek Basin) from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.closures: Park-wide moratorium on new fixed anchors (bolts/hangers) in wilderness areas - clean-climbing techniques only

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office / Search and Rescue - call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount - (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III Emergency Dept., (360) 424-4111); smaller Skagit Regional facility in Sedro-Woolley is somewhat closer geographically

routes.corrections: Wikipedia and some secondary sources describe the north face first ascent only as 'thirty years' after the 1932 peak FA (implying 1962); the primary AAC Journal account gives the precise date as July 8-11, 1961 (Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, Dave Hiser, Mike Swayne), which is used here as the better-sourced figure. The 'North Buttress'/'North Face' naming in this DB entry appears to conflate the original 1961 line with the harder, more popular 1984 'Stoddard Buttress' crest variant that later parties commonly climb; both are described here.

routes.rappels: Optional rappel from an established station on the West Ridge - faster and safer than downclimbing the 3rd/4th-class descent terrain.
objHaz: loose rock on the south-face traverse
routes.season: Jul-Sep, consistent with the range's short summer climbing window
routes.face: East Ridge (also described as the east edge of the Southeast Face)
routes.overview: Called 'probably the least popular route on the mountain,' this line climbs slabs to the saddle between Mount Terror and Mount Degenhardt, then follows a short, steep ridge - partly on the crest and partly wrapping onto the south/southeast face - to the summit.
routes.beta: Ascend slabs to the Terror-Degenhardt saddle, then climb the short but steep ridge above. The route goes up two pitches, traverses onto loose rock on the south (southeast) face, then regains the ridge for two more pitches with some 5th-class climbing mixed with easier scrambling - moderate enough that some parties climb it in mountaineering boots rather than rock shoes. Documented in Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, Vol. 3, 2nd ed. (pp. 106-107).
routes.approach: As for the West Ridge/standard approach: Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin/Crescent Creek Basin (multi-day, off-trail), then up slabs toward the Terror-Degenhardt col to gain the start of the ridge.
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge (3rd-4th class terrain), with an optional rappel from a fixed station for a faster, safer descent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb or scramble the West Ridge's 3rd-4th class terrain back toward Crescent Creek Cirque. Rappelling from an established station on the West Ridge is reported to be faster and safer than downclimbing the full length in marginal conditions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1-2: approach from Goodell Creek trailhead to a high camp in Crescent Creek Basin. Day 3: climb slabs to the Terror-Degenhardt saddle, ascend the East Ridge, descend via the West Ridge. Day 4-5: exit to trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ridge the way you came from either pitch pair; if already near the summit it may be quicker to continue and descend via the West Ridge line instead.
routes.turnaround: Given the remote, multi-day approach and sparse documentation of this line, set a conservative turnaround time and be prepared to reverse the route rather than push into unfamiliar terrain in poor visibility.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (Jul-Aug) offers the most settled weather and driest rock for the loose south-face traverse section; September remains feasible with care for early snow.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in this backcountry area; carry a satellite messenger/PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices given the low 5th-class difficulty - small-to-medium cams and stoppers plus a few slings; some parties climb the route largely in sturdy mountaineering boots.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal technical protection is needed; the primary challenges are route-finding and loose rock on the south-face traverse rather than hard climbing.
gear: rope for the short technical pitches and descent rappel
whatToBring: multi-day bivy/camp gear for the approach
route photos/beta in advance, since this line is sparsely documented
proTips: This is the least-documented, least-popular of Terror's routes - expect route-finding challenges and to rely on Beckey's guidebook description rather than online trip reports.
Because the difficulty stays in the low 5th class, many parties get by in mountaineering boots rather than swapping to rock shoes.
watchOut: Loose rock on the traverse section where the route crosses from the ridge crest onto the south/southeast face.
knownHazards: Loose rock underfoot during the south-face traverse between the two ridge sections.
The same remote, off-trail, multi-day approach shared by all of Terror's routes.

pitchDetail.notes: Slabs to the Terror-Degenhardt saddle, then the initial steep ridge section
Move onto the south/southeast face on loose rock to bypass a ridge obstacle
Regain the ridge crest for the final two pitches to the summit

blurb: North Gardner Mountain (8,956 ft) is the highest point in Okanogan County and in the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness, standing in the Methow Mountains, a subrange of the North Cascades east of the Cascade Crest. It is joined by a high connecting ridge to neighboring Gardner Mountain (8,898 ft) about a mile to the southeast; both peaks were named by Winthrop founder Guy Waring after Isabella Gardner of Boston. The mountain is not a technical rock climb but a long, remote backcountry scramble reached via a 10+ mile approach up the Wolf Creek valley to subalpine Gardner Meadows.
hierarchyNote: Name, coordinates (48.515°N/-120.502°W) and elevation (8,956 ft) are confirmed correct and cross-checked (Wikipedia, PeakVisor, SummitPost, Peakbagger all agree). However, the parent grouping "Washington Pass" is geographically loose: North Gardner Mountain sits roughly 10-11 km (6-7 mi) east-southeast of Washington Pass itself, in the separate Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness / Methow Mountains subrange, and is reached via the Wolf Creek Trailhead near Winthrop — not from the Washington Pass highway corridor that gives access to the granite spires (Liberty Bell Group, Silver Star, Early Winters Spires) the "Washington Pass" climbing area name usually denotes. This peak's one route is also an unroped Class 2-3 scramble/mountaineering objective, not technical rock climbing. Worth a hierarchy review, but returning full research under the given id as instructed.

routes.overview: A long, remote Class 2-3 alpine scramble approached from the south via the Wolf Creek valley and Gardner Meadows, gaining the summit ridge through a bowl below Point 8487 rather than via any technical rock line.
routes.beta: From a backcountry camp at Gardner Meadows (or, for a combined trip with Gardner Mountain, a high bivy near Point 8487), climbers work up scree and talus into the bowl below Point 8487, then make an exposed but sub-Class-3 traverse across to the North Gardner summit block. The line is entirely off-trail cross-country travel with no fixed protection, no established cairned route, and route-finding around cliff bands required. Most parties combine it with Gardner Mountain in a single overnight or two-night trip.
routes.approach: Drive to the signed Wolf Creek Trailhead (FS Trail #527) in the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness: from Winthrop, cross the Methow River bridge on the south end of town and turn onto Twin Lakes Road (county road 9120); after 1.5 mi turn onto Wolf Creek Road (county road 1131); after 4.5 mi turn onto gravel Left Fork Wolf Creek Road (county road 1145); after 0.25 mi continue straight onto gravel Forest Service Road 5505 for about 4.5 mi to the signed trailhead. From the trailhead (~2,923 ft), follow Wolf Creek Trail #527 roughly 10.3 miles up the valley — through extensive standing dead timber from the 2021 Cedar Creek Fire for much of the first 8 miles — to subalpine Gardner Meadows (~5,700 ft). Leave the trail on the west side of the small stream draining Gardner Mountain and climb cross-country, staying west of the stream, to a small meadow basin at about 6,200 ft, then work up and around cliff bands toward the ridge and the bowl below Point 8487; this adds roughly 1.5-2 additional miles and ~3,000+ ft of gain beyond the trail end.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: back down through the bowl below Point 8487 to Gardner Meadows and out the Wolf Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same Class 2-3 terrain used on ascent back into the bowl below Point 8487, staying well back from cliff bands on the way down to the meadow basin at ~6,200 ft. From there descend cross-country back to Gardner Meadows and rejoin Wolf Creek Trail #527 for the ~10.3-mile hike out to the trailhead. Parties traversing between Gardner Mountain and North Gardner can either stay on the connecting ridge (the 'Gardner Traverse,' reported to involve tricky exposed scrambling) or take the safer option of dropping to about 7,400 ft below the cliff bands to bypass the harder ridge section.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as an overnight (2-day) or two-night backpack: Day 1 — drive to Wolf Creek Trailhead and hike ~10.3 miles up Wolf Creek Trail #527 to camp at Gardner Meadows. Day 2 — climb cross-country to the bowl below Point 8487 (often summiting Gardner Mountain first), traverse/bivy near Point 8487 (a flattened-scree bivy site), then continue to the North Gardner summit before returning to camp or hiking out. Strong parties sometimes combine the whole round trip, reported at up to ~27 miles and ~8,100 ft of cumulative gain when both Gardner and North Gardner are climbed together, into a very long single push.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward and non-technical: reverse course down the bowl/scree below Point 8487 back to the meadow basin and Gardner Meadows, then follow the maintained Wolf Creek Trail back to the trailhead. No fixed ropes, rappels, or technical rock moves are required to bail from any point on the route.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach (20+ miles round trip car-to-car with no cell coverage in the Wolf Creek valley), set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather rather than pushing for the summit — North Cascades east-slope afternoon thunderstorms are a real factor in summer, and the cross-country bowl/ridge terrain is much harder to navigate safely in the dark or in a storm than the maintained trail sections.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions run July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the upper basin and ridge; the approach trail's wildflowers (arrowleaf balsamroot, lupine, avalanche lilies) peak in late spring/early summer just after snowmelt. Earlier-season attempts can encounter lingering snow on the upper mountain's north-facing terrain.
routes.face: Southwest Slopes / South Ridge, gained via the bowl below Point 8487
objHaz: standing dead timber/snags (2021 Cedar Creek Fire burn)
limited water in the upper basin
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is used on this route — it is climbed as an unroped Class 2-3 scramble. Trekking poles are widely recommended for the steep, loose scree sections; an ice axe may be prudent if lingering snow remains on north-facing terrain earlier in the season.
routes.proNeeds: None — parties do not typically place protection or use a rope on this line; it is treated as advanced scrambling terrain throughout.
gear: map and compass or GPS with route track
whatToBring: overnight backpacking/camping gear for the Gardner Meadows or Point 8487 bivy
extra water and a filter/treatment (little to no reliable water above Gardner Meadows)
layers for cold, exposed ridge conditions and sudden weather changes
headlamp (navigation through the burned lower trail is reported to be difficult after dark)
bear spray (black bear activity reported in the valley)
proTips: Camp at Gardner Meadows (or bivy near Point 8487, described as having flattened scree suitable for sleeping) to break the long approach into a manageable multi-day trip and allow an early climb on summit day.
Gardner Mountain and North Gardner are commonly climbed together in one push from a Gardner Meadows camp.
Take the safer traverse option that drops to about 7,400 ft below the cliff bands rather than the exposed 'Gardner Traverse' ridge crest when moving between the two summits.
Do the final scree/talus slopes in the cool of morning — the loose, marble-sized rock underfoot is easier to manage with firmer overnight conditions.
watchOut: Extensive standing dead timber from the 2021 Cedar Creek Fire along the first ~8 miles of the Wolf Creek Trail — falling snags are a real hazard, especially in wind.
The final approach slope below the summit ridge is described as a 'brutal' scree slope of loose, marble-sized rock with poor footing.
The Wolf Creek Trail becomes hard to follow through burned, regrowing terrain after dark.
No dependable water sources in the upper basin — carry enough for a long summit day.
Wildlife encounters (black bear, cougar, and reports of wolves/coyotes) have been noted in the valley.
knownHazards: Approach travels 8+ miles through severely fire-damaged forest (2021 Cedar Creek Fire, ~90% intense burn) with ongoing snag-fall risk.
The summit scramble crosses loose scree/talus and involves route-finding around cliff bands with no cairned trail.
The ridge traverse connecting Gardner and North Gardner has an exposed 'harder' option along the crest and a longer but safer option dropping below cliffs near 7,400 ft.
This is a very long, remote day with no cell coverage — self-rescue capability is required.

pitchDetail.notes: ~10.3 mi one-way on maintained Wolf Creek Trail #527, ~2,900 ft to ~5,700 ft, through extensive fire-damaged forest for the first 8 miles.

pitchDetail.pitch: Gardner Meadows to meadow basin / ridge approach
pitchDetail.notes: Off-trail, staying west of the stream draining Gardner Mountain, reaching a small meadow basin at ~6,200 ft before working around cliff bands toward the ridge.

climate.typical: East of the Cascade Crest, the Methow Valley/Sawtooth area has a drier, more continental climate than the west-side Cascades, with sunnier summers and colder, snowier winters.
climate.spring: Snow lingers into June on the upper mountain and approach trail; wildflowers peak on lower Wolf Creek Trail in late spring as snow recedes.
climate.summer: The main climbing season (Jul-Sep); generally dry and warm, though afternoon thunderstorms can build over the Cascade Crest and East Slopes.
climate.fall: Cools quickly by late September with the first snow possible on the upper peak; good early-fall window with settled weather and fewer bugs.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and full winter mountaineering conditions; access road may be snowed in well below the trailhead.

blurb: Argonaut Peak (8,457 ft) is a granite summit on the crest of the Stuart Range in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), sitting on the ridgeline between Colchuck Peak and Sherpa Peak, about 1.7 miles west of Isolation Lake and 1.8 miles east of Mount Stuart. The peak is a large bastion of clean granite belonging to the Mount Stuart batholith, with a west (true) and southeast summit of nearly identical height, and offers several moderate alpine rock lines on its south, southeast, northeast, and northwest sides. It was first climbed in September 1955 via the south route by Lex Maxwell, Bob McCall, and Bill Prater.
hierarchyNote: None — coordinates (47.470119, -120.861477) and elevation (8,457 ft, confirmed against Wikipedia's 8,457 ft NAVD88 figure at 47°28'12"N 120°51'41"W) match Argonaut Peak exactly, and the Stuart Range/Enchantments parent area is correct (peak sits between Colchuck and Sherpa Peaks). Note some sources (Peakbagger-derived aggregations) cite 8,453 ft, likely an older NGVD29 datum figure — 8,457 ft is retained as the better-sourced value.

routes.overview: A direct, sustained 300-foot 5.6 line up clean granite on the south face, immediately below the main (west) summit — the most technical way to reach the top from the south side.
routes.beta: Start in the rightmost and larger of two short left-facing corners on the wall base; follow blocky, generally solid rock 'to the summit' with several viable variations up the face. Watch for a few loose blocks low on the route. The grade (5.6) is an old-school rating and climbs sustained, comparable to roughly 5.9-5.10- by modern gym standards.
routes.bestSeason: South-facing and quick to dry out, but the approach gully/creek crossings can still hold snow into early-mid July in a heavy snow year; driest and most reliable conditions are July-September.
routes.approach: Approached from the south via Beverly Creek Trailhead (Teanaway/North Fork Teanaway Road, FS 9737): follow Beverly Creek Trail to a pass into the Fourth Creek drainage, then descend Fourth Creek Trail to Ingalls Creek, cross the creek, and bushwhack/scramble up talus and open slopes on the peak's south side directly to the base of the wall.
gear: Standard alpine trad rack to 3 in
Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: Ten essentials
Extra water for the long, exposed south-side approach
watchOut: Potentially loose blocks near the bottom of the face
Long, strenuous, partly bushwhack approach from Ingalls Creek
knownHazards: Loose rock low on the route
Extended off-trail travel through brush and talus on approach

routes.overview: A long, mostly-solid alpine rock ridge climbing to Argonaut's southeast spire (a false summit of nearly equal height), requiring a rappel into the notch to continue to the true (west) summit.
routes.beta: An interesting, less-traveled rock route up the SE ridge to the SE spire and, via a rappel into the intervening notch, on to the true summit. The lower pitches are blocky, somewhat awkward, and lichen-covered, making protection tricky in spots; upper pitches improve on cleaner rock.
routes.bestSeason: Best once the approach snow and moat below the ridge have melted out, typically mid-July through September.
routes.approach: Best reached via Colchuck Col from the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead (Eightmile Creek Road/FS 7601) and Colchuck Lake, traversing to gain the base of the southeast ridge — this is preferred over the long, strenuous Ingalls Creek bushwhack approach from the south, which is not recommended.
routes.rappels: 2 single-rope rappels (or 1 double-rope rappel) into the notch between the southeast spire and the true summit
watchOut: Lower pitches are blocky, awkward, and hard to protect
Heavy lichen coverage reduces friction and gear placements
SE spire is a false summit — a rappel into the notch is required to reach the true summit

routes.overview: A mixed rock-and-snow ridge/gully line gained from Colchuck Col, climbing exposed ledges and a snow-filled couloir to the northeast ridge before short technical rock and snow slopes lead to the summit block.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Col, traverse an exposed ridge and rock ledges to a narrow, snow-filled ~50-degree couloir; a short section of easy 5th-class rock (about two moves of 5.4) with a 4th-class scramble gains the northeast ridge/gully system, followed by a steep (~55-degree) snow finger and lower-angle snow on the northeast face to the summit blocks.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed as a snow-and-rock route; best when the couloir and northeast face still hold consolidated snow (June into early July) rather than late-season scree and bare rock.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead (FS 7601), hike to Colchuck Lake and climb to Colchuck Col (~8,050 ft), then traverse toward Argonaut Peak along the connecting ridge to gain the route.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route back over Colchuck Col to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the summit blocks and northeast face snow/rock back to the couloir and ridge, re-cross to Colchuck Col, then descend past Colchuck Lake to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; expect roughly 5-6 hours for the descent.

routes.corrections: The given route name 'Northeast Ridge' does not correspond to a distinctly named route on Mountain Project (which lists 'Northeast Couloir' as the technical snow/ice line on this side); it is documented in multiple trip reports as a moderate mixed ridge/gully scramble reached via Colchuck Col, distinct from the harder Northeast Couloir. Stats above (gain/distance/timing) are round-trip totals from a specific documented ascent, not one-way approach figures.

routes.overview: A 6-8 pitch alpine rock climb up the blocky northwest arete of Argonaut Peak, gained via a snow finger from the north basin, with generally solid but lichen-covered granite near the crest.
routes.beta: From the north basin below the peak, climb a snow finger (loose/dirty rock in late season) to a notch at the base of the northwest arete, then follow straightforward route-finding up blocky, generally solid Class 4 to 5.6 rock; a standout pitch 5 climbs a steep corner on good holds, with a choice of corner systems higher up (the right-hand option is easier). Black lichen on the granite becomes extremely slick when wet.
routes.bestSeason: Best once the north-basin snow finger has firmed up or melted out, typically July-August; September ascents report looser late-season snow, mud, and moat issues.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead (FS 7601), follow the Stuart Lake trail about 2 miles to a junction, continue to a meadow, then follow Mountaineer Creek (east fork) through brush and a large boulder field to the north basin below the peak, staying low and right through slide-alder to minimize bushwhacking.
routes.descent: Descend east from the summit through a notch and down the northeast face via a series of rappels to the Argonaut-Colchuck col.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/traverse east through a notch onto the northeast face; make one single-rope rappel to the east, traverse skier's left (north) through a loose gully, then two more single-rope rappels to reach the slopes near the Argonaut-Colchuck col, from which parties descend to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead. Expect roughly 4 hours off the summit to the col.
whatToBring: Overnight/bivy gear in case of weather delay
Extra layers — conditions can shift quickly to graupel/snow/rain
proTips: Stay low and traverse right (skier's right) through the boulder field/slide alder on approach to minimize bushwhacking
Expect to pitch out more sections (7-8 pitches) rather than the 5 an efficient party might use, to manage rope drag and communication in variable weather
watchOut: Black lichen-covered granite is extremely slick when wet ('climbing on rock covered in wet lettuce')
Rapid weather changes bring graupel, snow, and rain even in summer
Late-season snow finger can be loose, dirty, or moated out
Pack rats reported on cliff bands around 8,500 ft
knownHazards: Slick lichen in wet conditions
Fast-moving alpine weather
Loose rock in the late-season snow finger

blurb: Bear Mountain (7,931 ft) is a remote summit in the Chilliwack Range (grouped within the broader Skagit Range) of the North Cascades, inside North Cascades National Park near the US/Canada border in Whatcom County. Its roughly 2,000-foot north face, cut from Skagit gneiss and granodiorite, holds some of the most committing and least-visited alpine rock climbing in the Cascades. Fred Beckey and Mark Fielding made the first ascent of the north buttress in July 1967 via a glacier couloir; Alan Kearney and Bobby Knight added a harder, fully-direct line up the entire buttress in September 1980.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey & Mark Fielding, July 14-15, 1967 (crux originally aided at A4; freed by later parties)
objHaz: rockfall in the approach glacier couloir
wet/slick slabs when rock is damp
routes.overview: Gains the upper north buttress via a steep glacier couloir left of the rock, then climbs seven sustained pitches of cracks, corners and an airy face to the top of the buttress.
routes.beta: The original 1967 Beckey-Fielding line, catalogued as the 'North Face Buttress' in the Cascade Alpine Guide (or 'North Face' in Beckey's Alpine Select). Parties gain the buttress crest via a roughly 1,100-ft glacier couloir left of the rock (about 3.5 hrs, dodging rockfall and crossing exposed pebble-strewn slabs), then climb seven pitches: a steep 5.9 corner, 5.6 crest climbing, a 5.8 pitch with an off-width to a ledge (the 1967 party's bivy site), the 5.10a off-width crux, 5.7 blocks and cracks, airy 5.9 face climbing on twin cracks, and a final 5.7 blocky pitch to the buttress top. The FA party used aid (A4) through the crux; it has since been freed at 5.10a.
routes.approach: From the Chilliwack Lake Road trailhead in British Columbia (historically reached by boat across Chilliwack Lake), or via the longer Hannegan Pass Trail on the US side to avoid the border crossing, follow the Chilliwack River Trail toward Bear Creek (about 5 hrs from the BC trailhead), then leave the trail and climb the steep, brushy Bear Creek 'high route' through timber and huckleberry to meadows below Bear Mountain's north side, reaching a saddle camp at roughly 6,480 ft — about 9 miles and 4,500 ft of gain, ~9 hours from the BC trailhead.
routes.descent: 3rd/4th class walk-off down the west side of the south ridge back to the saddle-camp basin, about 1 hour.
routes.descentText: From the top of the buttress, descend westward on easier ground along the south-ridge side of the peak, working down through a gully system back to the basin beneath the saddle camp; roughly 1 hour, no rappels required.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in via the Chilliwack River Trail to Bear Creek and up the high route to saddle camp (~6,480 ft). Day 2: climb the glacier couloir and seven-pitch buttress to the top, then descend to camp. Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat becomes difficult once established on the buttress crest above the couloir; the easiest bail is back down the glacier couloir before committing to the upper rock pitches.
routes.turnaround: Given the remote, multi-day approach and limited retreat once on the upper buttress, set a firm turnaround time based on weather and remaining daylight to ensure a safe descent to the couloir before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-to-late summer (July-September) once the approach couloir/glacier has consolidated and the brushy Bear Creek approach has dried out; the first ascent and known repeat climbs were all done between mid-July and early September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Bear Creek drainage or on the peak; this is a remote wilderness area abutting the Canadian border.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of nuts and cams to about 3 inches (doubles in mid-size cams), plus extra slings; a 60m single rope is sufficient.
routes.proNeeds: Standard rack to roughly 3 inches covers the crack and corner systems; the crux off-width pitch wants larger gear.

multi-day camping/bivy gear for the approach
approach shoes for brush
passport if approaching via Canada

Camping at the ~6,480 ft saddle sets up a single long summit day

Wet, slick rock is common in this weather-exposed range

Crevasse hazard crossing the approach glacier
Very remote setting far from any rescue access

routes.fa: Alan Kearney & Bobby Knight, September 1980 (entire buttress, ~2.5 days); free ascent by Bryan Burdo & Yann Merrand, 1985
routes.rappels: None — walk-off descent, single rope sufficient
objHaz: loose ice blocks in the ice moat at the base
unstable, glacially-scoured rock low on the route
routes.face: North Buttress (direct line up the full/lower buttress)
routes.overview: A more direct and sustained line than the original Beckey route, climbing the entire north buttress from its toe on the glacier's edge in 21 pitches, joining and finishing on the classic upper buttress.
routes.beta: Established by Alan Kearney and Bobby Knight over 2.5 days in September 1980, the Direct North Buttress adds roughly seven difficult 5.10 pitches up the previously-unclimbed lower buttress before joining Beckey and Fielding's 1967 line on the upper buttress. Standout lower-buttress features include a 300-ft dihedral with a roof, a 130-ft hand-and-fist crack, and an off-width; the route finishes via the shared upper-buttress pitches to the crest.
routes.approach: Same multi-day approach as the North Buttress — via the Chilliwack River Trail/Bear Creek high route (or the longer Hannegan Pass Trail on the US side) to a saddle camp on Bear Mountain's northwest side at about 6,480 ft. From the saddle, descend steep scree/snow toward the approach glacier; the Direct North Buttress toe sits on the glacier's right side — locate slabs feeding into the lower corner system (crampons and axe helpful for the 60-90 ft down from the col).
routes.descent: Reverse the approach: descend snow slopes and pick through cliff bands back to the basin beneath the saddle; single-rope walk-off, no rappels required.
routes.descentText: From the top of the buttress, downclimb and walk snow/talus slopes on the peak's west side, navigating cliff bands back down to the basin below the col and saddle camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: approach to saddle camp (~6,480 ft). Days 2-3: climb the 21-pitch buttress (historically done in about 2.5 days with a bivy), then descend to camp. Day 4: hike out.
routes.bail: The lower seven pitches offer limited retreat with sparse protection; the easiest bail is before committing above the roof/dihedral section low on the route.
routes.turnaround: Because the route is long (21 pitches) and rescue is far away, set a clear turnaround based on pace through the lower pitches to avoid finishing the upper buttress after dark or in worsening weather.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late summer (July-September), once the approach glacier is stable and ice/snow has cleared the base slabs; the first ascent was climbed in early September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; a remote wilderness setting abutting the Canadian border.
routes.detailedRack: A full rack with cams up to BD #4, doubles of mid-range cams, and numerous slings for the traversing and sparsely-protected pitches; a single rope is acceptable since the descent is a walk-off.
routes.proNeeds: Doubles of mid-size cams cover the crack systems on the lower buttress; a #4-size cam protects the wider sections and off-width.
gear: rack of cams to BD #4 (doubles of mid-range sizes)
whatToBring: glacier travel gear
multi-day camping/bivy gear
approach shoes for brush and scree

blurb: Mount Chaval (7,127 ft) is a craggy, remote summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, roughly 15 miles northeast of Darrington in Skagit County, WA, just southwest of Snowking Mountain. It is built of the Chaval pluton, a Late Cretaceous pyroxene-bearing diorite/tonalite, and carries two subsidiary summits (East Peak and Middle Peak, both 7,040 ft). Despite modest technical difficulty on its standard line, it is one of the more rarely-visited Cascade peaks because of its long, brushy, arduous approach; it was first climbed on September 20, 1946 by Richard Merritt with Keith and Ruth Rankin.

routes.overview: The West Route follows the Jug Creek drainage past Bluff and Cliff Lakes to Mount Chaval's west ridge, then climbs steep heather, talus, and a snow ramp/gully to the summit pyramid — the peak's easiest and most commonly used line, though the long, brushy approach makes it feel harder than its Class 2-3 rating suggests.
routes.beta: Classified a 'Basic Alpine' climb by The Mountaineers, roughly 10 miles round trip with about 4,600 ft of elevation gain from the historic Grade Creek Road trailhead; a detailed trip report covering a camp-based west-ridge/northwest-face variation logged 14 miles and 6,400 ft round trip. The crux is not technical difficulty but the arduous, often overgrown approach; the final few hundred feet to the summit involve a steep snow ramp/gully flanked by rock bands, commonly climbed with an ice axe and crampons in early-to-mid season.
objHaz: Long, brushy, poorly-marked approach through the Jug Creek/Bluff Lake basin
Steep snow/ice on the summit gully and ramp
Currently no vehicle access via Suiattle River Road/Grade Creek Road (washout closure)

road.name: Suiattle River Road (FSR 26) to Grade Creek Road (FSR 2640)

As of 2026, FSR 26 is closed to vehicles at MP 4 and FSR 2640 is closed along its entire length (to Lake Tupso Trailhead), so this approach currently requires substantial extra road-walking/biking distance beyond the historic trailhead.

routes.approach: From Darrington, drive SR 530 east/north and cross the Sauk River onto Suiattle River Road (FSR 26); continue to Grade Creek Road (FSR 2640) and its junction with spur road FSR 2643 (historic trailhead, ~2,250 ft). Hike the abandoned spur road roughly 2 miles to a sharp switchback (~3,150 ft), where a rough fisherman's path leaves the roadbed, crosses several branches of Jug (Found) Creek, and climbs the west bank to a small basin near 3,700 ft. From there, ascend snow-covered or brushy slopes past Bluff Lake (~4,237 ft) and Cliff Lake to a saddle on Chaval's west ridge (roughly 5,400-5,900 ft depending on variation). NOTE: as of 2026, FSR 26 is closed to vehicles at MP 4 and FSR 2640 (Grade Creek Road) is closed in its entirety through at least January 2028 due to flood damage — this adds significant road-walking distance to the approach; verify current Forest Service closure status before attempting.
routes.descent: Reverse the west ridge/heather-slope ascent route back through the Jug Creek basin to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the snow ramp/gully and the heather-and-talus slopes back to the west-ridge saddle, then drop back into the Jug Creek basin past Cliff and Bluff Lakes, retracing the fisherman's path to the old spur road and out to the Grade Creek Road trailhead. Trip reports note the descent is markedly faster once the route through the brush is known (roughly 5 hours versus 8-9 hours on the way in).
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a 2-day trip: Day 1 — drive to the (currently closed/extended) trailhead and hike in via the Jug Creek basin, Bluff Lake, and Cliff Lake to a camp on a heather bench near 6,100 ft below the west ridge (roughly 8.5 hours car-to-camp per trip reports); Day 2 — climb the west ridge to the summit (about 1 hour from camp on the easy scramble section) and return to the car (roughly 5 hours). Strong parties have also done it car-to-car in a long single day.
routes.bail: There are no fixed rappel stations on the standard line; retreat is by reversing the ascent. From the summit pyramid, downclimb the snow ramp/gully before it softens in afternoon sun; from the west ridge, the heather slopes provide a walk-off in most conditions. Once committed to the Jug Creek basin there is no shorter bail option — retreat means re-descending the full approach.
routes.turnaround: Because the summit snow ramp/gully softens and becomes hazardous in afternoon sun, and both approach and descent take many hours through remote, trailless terrain, parties should set a firm turnaround time based on that day's conditions and remaining daylight, and commit to it regardless of proximity to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly June through August, when a stable snowpack still covers the approach basin and the final gully, easing travel on terrain that is otherwise slow, brushy, and moss-covered; later in summer the snow ramp can bare out to unstable talus and heather. An ice axe and crampons are typically needed for much of the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage is reported through the Jug Creek/Bluff Lake approach basin or on the summit ridge; a satellite communicator is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard West Route scramble. Carry an ice axe and crampons for the snow ramp/gully sections below the summit pyramid, especially in early-mid season. A helmet is recommended given loose, moss-covered rock. Trekking poles help on the long brushy/heather approach. Parties attempting the harder North Ridge alternate (a distinct, separate line, not this route) should bring a light rack of cams and nuts for low-5th-class rock.
routes.proNeeds: Standard scramble protection only: ice axe and crampons for the snow sections, no fixed rock protection required on the normal west-ridge/west-face line.
whatToBring: Insect repellent (heavy bug pressure reported in the Jug Creek basin)
Extra water (limited reliable sources along the ridge)
Brush-clearing tool (loppers/saw) for overgrown sections
Detailed map/GPS track for the Jug Creek basin, where route-finding is difficult
Overnight camping gear if splitting the trip into two days
proTips: Go with significant snowcover on the upper mountain (typically June) to bury the loose talus/heather and make the final snowfields straightforward rather than fighting moss-covered rock.
Budget a full day (or an overnight) for the approach alone — the Jug Creek/Bluff Lake basin brush and boulder-hopping is notoriously slow.
Camp on the heather bench near 6,100 ft below the west ridge to split the trip into a manageable two-day outing.
Check current Forest Service road-closure status before you go — the 2026 Suiattle River Road/Grade Creek Road washout closures have significantly lengthened the approach.
watchOut: Steep, side-hilling traverse sections with real exposure that are easy to underestimate given the 'Class 2-3' rating.
Afternoon sun turns the summit snow ramp mushy and unstable — climb it early.
Heavy insect activity reported in the Jug Creek basin in summer.
Poor, unmarked route-finding through brush and boulder fields; parties have gotten off-route.
knownHazards: Long, arduous, brushy approach through the Jug Creek/Bluff Lake basin with minimal trail.
Steep snow/ice on the final summit pyramid gully and ramp, often above exposed rock bands.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer.

blurb: Guye Peak (5,168 ft) is a small, steep summit of highly fractured, crumbly rhyolite rising directly above I-90 at Snoqualmie Pass in the Washington Cascades, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness roughly 50 miles east of Seattle. Its west face — plainly visible from the interstate — and north ridge give some of the most accessible multi-pitch rock climbing and class 3-4 scrambling near Seattle, though the rock is notoriously loose outside a few solid sections (rockfall/breakage has caused documented injuries and fatalities on the peak). Guye Peak was first climbed on October 6, 1912 by Charles Hazlehurst, Hector Abel, Paul Dubuar, and Mary Hard.
hierarchyNote: The peak's own data (name/coords/elevation) checks out and is internally consistent across Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, and Peakbagger. However, the given parentArea "Central-East Cascades, Wenatchee, & Leavenworth" looks geographically mismatched: Guye Peak sits in King County on the WEST side of Snoqualmie Pass (I-90 Exit 52), and Mountain Project itself files it under "Central-West Cascades & Seattle," not the east-side Wenatchee/Leavenworth region (Chelan/Kittitas county area well to the northeast). Recommend re-parenting under a west-side/Snoqualmie Pass Cascades bucket rather than Wenatchee/Leavenworth.

routes.fa: Dave Hiser and Mike Borghoff, 1960 (first winter ascent by Kit Lewis, 1975)
routes.rappels: Rappel from a tree anchor on the east side, then a short gully to bypass the true summit (single-rope rap; length depends on tree used)
objHaz: Ski-area avalanche control blasting nearby in winter
routes.overview: A 6-pitch, Grade III line up Guye Peak's obvious west face, climbing low-quality slabs to 'Lunch Ledge,' then the exposed 5.8 'Improbable Traverse' pitch (the route's namesake and best rock), before finishing up a 4th-class ramp.
routes.beta: Also known as 'Improbable Traverse' (its Mountain Project name) — the two names describe the same climb. Rock is generally poor except on the signature traverse pitch, which offers the route's only solid climbing. A November 2021 rockfall collapsed a 30x40 ft section of the traverse; as of a July 2022 trip report the pitch requires an unprotected 5.9-10- downclimb roughly 20-30+ ft from an old pin, and was assessed as 'not recommended' in that state — check current conditions before attempting.
routes.approach: Park considerately along Ober Strasse Road in the small Snoqualmie Pass community (off I-90 Exit 52) and walk up through the neighborhood — this crosses private property, so be discreet and respectful of residents, some of whom have objected to climber traffic. Continue up the talus cone beneath the west face, then scramble a couple hundred feet of loose, mossy, dirty slabs to the start of the roped climbing.
routes.descent: Rappel from a tree on the east side and downclimb a short gully to bypass the true summit's exposed, poor-rock final ridge.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, traverse north over two subsummits rather than continuing over the loose, exposed final ridge to the true summit. Most parties rappel from a tree anchor on the east side of the peak, then descend a short gully that bypasses the true summit before regaining the standard descent trail toward the Guye-Snoqualmie saddle.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on the lower pitches by rappelling/downclimbing back toward the talus cone from natural (tree) anchors. Once committed across the crux traverse pitch, it is generally easier to continue to the tree-rappel/gully descent on the east side than to reverse the exposed traverse.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux traverse pitch is currently degraded by 2021 rockfall damage and the descent requires downclimbing exposed, loose terrain, turn back if the rock is wet, if daylight is limited, or if any party member is not confident downclimbing loose 5th-class terrain — Cascade afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer and this route has no quick retreat once past the traverse.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season runs roughly June through October once the approach slabs have dried out; the route is best attempted in the June-September window. The west-facing wall gets intense afternoon sun, so many parties start early; avoid climbing when the rock is wet, since the already-loose rock becomes significantly more hazardous.
routes.detailedRack: A standard medium-sized alpine rack (small-to-mid cams and nuts) covers most of the route. Carry a small nut or cam specifically for building the anchor at the far (north) end of the Improbable Traverse pitch. Old fixed pitons protect part of the traverse but should not be trusted as bomber protection given the loose rock and 2021 rockfall damage.
routes.proNeeds: Mid-size alpine rack; small nut/cam reserved for the traverse-pitch anchor; old fixed pins exist on the traverse but are unreliable.
gear: Mid-size alpine rack (small-to-mid cams and nuts)
Small nut/cam for the traverse-pitch anchor
whatToBring: Sunscreen (intense afternoon sun on this west-facing wall)
Approach shoes for the talus/slab scramble
Water (none available on route)
Light rain shell
proTips: Start early to beat the intense afternoon sun on this west-facing wall.
Wear a helmet for the entire route — rockfall is a real, documented hazard here.
Bring a small nut/cam specifically for the anchor at the far end of the traverse pitch.
Descend by rappelling from the tree anchor on the east side and skirting the true summit via a short gully rather than continuing over exposed, poor-quality ridge rock.
watchOut: Rock quality is generally poor and 'very loose' except on the traverse pitch — expect loose blocks and dirty, mossy slabs on the approach and lower pitches.

A November 2021 rockfall collapsed a 30x40 ft section of the traverse pitch; a July 2022 trip report found the pitch now requires an unprotected 5.9-10- downclimb and assessed the route as 'not recommended' in that condition.
Ski-area avalanche control blasting can occur on nearby terrain in winter — contact Summit at Snoqualmie/Alpental if climbing in that season.
The approach via Ober Strasse Road crosses private residential property; some residents have reported friction with climbers.
knownHazards: Loose, fractured rhyolite rock throughout most of the route.
Documented rockfall/breakage hazard on Guye Peak, including fatalities attributed to rockfall on the peak historically.
The crux traverse pitch has been structurally degraded since a November 2021 rockfall event.

pitchDetail.notes: Roughly 300 ft of low-quality, low-angle slab/4th-5th class terrain leading to the 'Lunch Ledge' belay.

The route's namesake pitch and best rock; climb ~5.8 moves out right, protected by old fixed pitons. Since a Nov 2021 rockfall, the far end now involves an unprotected 5.9-10- downclimb — verify current condition before committing.
Downclimb off the far end of the traverse to a 4th-class ramp, then ascend the ramp toward the summit approach; many parties bypass the loose, exposed true-summit ridge.

road.status: Paved neighborhood streets in the Snoqualmie Pass community; roadside parking only
road.driveNote: From I-90 take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass/Alpental) into the small Snoqualmie Pass residential community; park considerately along Ober Strasse and walk up through the neighborhood to reach the base of the talus cone below the west face. climate.typical: Snoqualmie Pass sits directly on the Cascade crest and receives some of the heaviest precipitation/snowfall of any highway pass in the Lower 48; summers are comparatively dry and mild, winters wet and snowy.
climate.spring: Often still wet with lingering snow into June; approach slabs may still be damp/mossy.
climate.summer: Driest and most reliable season for rock; the west face bakes in intense afternoon sun.
climate.fall: Generally stable through September-October before fall storms return; days shorten quickly.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; this is a rock route not typically climbed in winter conditions.

access.closures: Approach crosses private residential streets (Ober Strasse) in the Snoqualmie Pass community — respect no-parking/no-trespassing requests from homeowners.

routes.rockGrade: 5.4 (max move on the Hidden Ridge variation; otherwise class 3-4)
routes.rappels: One ~30 ft rappel off a slung horn (Hidden Ridge variation) down into the notch between the north and middle summits
objHaz: Loose rock typical of Guye Peak
Exposure near the summit ridge and notches
routes.season: Jul-Sep for a snow-free class 3 scramble; also climbed as a winter/spring snow route with an ice axe
routes.overview: The technically easiest way up Guye Peak, gaining the north summit via a ridge scramble from the Cave Ridge/Guye saddle; the 'Hidden Ridge' is an undocumented-until-recently variation that swaps the standard descent gully for a short, more direct class 4/low-5th ridge behind a hidden outcropping.
routes.beta: Park at Alpental near the Snow Lake Trailhead and start up the unsigned Cave Ridge/Snoqualmie Mountain climbers' trail beginning about 100 ft south of the Snow Lake Trail. From a large clearing around 4000-4200 ft, climb to the saddle between Cave Ridge and Guye Peak, then turn right (north) and follow the ridge to Guye's north summit — class 3 in summer. The 'Hidden Ridge' variation continues from the north summit around an outcropping to a hidden ridge with one 5.4 move (otherwise class 4), rappelling ~30 ft off a slung horn to the notch between the north and middle summits; this was found to be a more direct, more enjoyable line than the standard gully descent in winter/spring conditions.
routes.approach: From the Alpental parking lot (Snow Lake Trailhead), walk toward the Snow Lake Trail and pick up an unsigned climbers' trail into the slide alder roughly 50-100 ft downhill/south of the signed trailhead. Follow this steep climbers' trail up toward Cave Ridge/Snoqualmie Mountain terrain to a clearing around 4000-4200 ft, then continue to the saddle between Cave Ridge and Guye Peak.
routes.descent: Standard: from the north summit, descend a gully ~60 ft below the summit to the left (east), continue down ~70 ft to the next gully, then follow it up to the notch between the north and middle summits, and reverse the ascent ridge back to the saddle and trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the north summit there are two ways down to the notch below: (1) the standard gully route — descend a steep gully beginning about 60 ft below the summit, down roughly 70 ft until reaching a second gully, then follow that gully up to the notch between the north and middle summits; or (2) the 'Hidden Ridge' — traverse around the summit to the west for about 100 ft to an outcropping, sling a horn, and rappel about 30 ft down to the same notch. From the notch, either continue toward the middle/south summits or reverse the ridge back to the Cave Ridge/Guye saddle and descend the climbers' trail to Alpental.
routes.bail: Reverse the ridge back to the Cave Ridge/Guye saddle and descend the climbers' trail to the Alpental trailhead; existing rappel rings in the notch between the north and middle summits allow a quick rappel if needed instead of downclimbing.
routes.turnaround: In winter/spring, turn back if the gullies show fresh wind-loaded snow or unstable conditions per the NWAC forecast; year-round, turn back if the party is not comfortable with exposed class 3-4 scrambling or if weather is deteriorating, since the ridge offers little shelter.
routes.bestSeason: Snow-free class 3 conditions are typical roughly July through September. The route is also a popular winter/spring scramble objective, but then the gullies fill with snow/ice and the climb becomes steeper and more serious, requiring an ice axe and check of the NWAC Snoqualmie Pass avalanche forecast beforehand.
routes.detailedRack: No technical gear is required in mid-to-late summer once the snow has melted — it's a class 3 scramble. In winter/spring conditions, carry an ice axe and a 30m light/glacier rope for the gullies, plus webbing to sling trees (and optionally 1-2 pickets). For the 'Hidden Ridge' variation, bring one long sling/webbing to sling the summit-block horn for the ~30 ft rappel, plus a couple of extra slings to extend the rappel to the pitch's edge.
routes.proNeeds: None needed in dry summer conditions; winter/spring parties should carry an ice axe, a light rope, and slings for tree/horn anchors.
gear: Long sling/webbing for the horn rappel (Hidden Ridge variation)
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the climbers' trail approach
Traction device (microspikes) for icy shoulder-season sections
Sun protection — open ridge with little shade
Sturdy scrambling shoes
proTips: In mid-to-late summer after the snow melts, the standard North Route gullies require no technical gear — just careful class-3 scrambling.

The 'Hidden Ridge' variation, hidden behind an outcropping just below and west of the north summit, is a fun, more direct class 4/low-5th alternative to the standard gully descent, with only one 5.4 move.
In winter/spring the gullies fill with snow and become notably steeper and more intimidating — carry an ice axe and a light rope.
Rappel rings already exist in the notch between the north and middle summits from prior parties.
watchOut: Class 3 terrain becomes considerably more serious in winter/spring when the gullies fill with snow — carry an ice axe and consider a rope.
Loose rock typical of Guye Peak is present in places.
Significant exposure exists near the summit ridge and the notches between summits; the standard gully descent is steep.
knownHazards: Loose rock typical of Guye Peak's fractured rhyolite.
Steep, snow-filled gullies in winter/spring create fall hazard and some avalanche potential.
Exposed ridge terrain connecting the north, middle, and south summits.

pitchDetail.notes: Climbers' trail from Alpental past the Snow Lake TH turnoff, up to a ~4000-4200 ft clearing, then to the Cave Ridge/Guye saddle.
From the saddle, turn right and follow the ridge to Guye's north summit; steep but only class 3 when snow-free. In winter/spring the terrain is icier and more serious.
From the north summit, traverse ~100 ft west around to an outcropping below the summit, sling the horn, and rappel ~30 ft to the notch between the north and middle summits.
Descend a gully ~60 ft below the north summit, down ~70 ft, then up the next gully to the same notch between north and middle summits.

climate.summer: Driest, most reliable season for the class-3 scramble.
climate.fall: Generally stable through September-October before storms return; days shorten quickly.
climate.winter: A recognized winter scramble objective at Snoqualmie Pass, but gullies become steep, icy, and avalanche-prone — check NWAC before going.
