blurb: Ed Wood Memorial Buttress is an informally named rock buttress in the Southern Pickets of the North Cascades (North Cascades National Park), sitting just north of the Himmelhorn on the northwest side of "The Blob" (also called The Rake). Like the rest of the Pickets it is composed of biotite gneiss, and rather than being a distinct summit it is a long committing ridge-line feature leading to The Blob's summit ridge. The one recorded route and several of its features (Bela Lugosi Traverse) carry a tongue-in-cheek Ed Wood horror-movie naming theme established by the 2006 first-ascent party.
hierarchyNote: Ed Wood Memorial Buttress is not a separately named, independent summit in USGS/Peakbagger data — it is an informally named rock buttress/sub-feature on the northwest (left) side of "The Blob" (aka The Rake), located just north of Himmelhorn in the Southern Pickets. The parent area "Southern Pickets" and the given coordinates (48.7716, -121.28356) are correct and match Mountain Project's area listing exactly. Because this is a buttress feature rather than a true summit, there is no single sourced "peak elevation" — Mountain Project lists 7,337 ft as the area's GPS reference point, while the ridge the route tops out on (The Blob) has a summit around 7,840 ft per StephAbegg.com. No red flags beyond this naming/classification nuance; proceed with the given id.

objHaz: No fixed anchors anywhere on route
Loose/complex alpine rock typical of the Pickets
Remote, committing position far from any quick escape
routes.face: Right edge of Ed Wood Memorial Buttress, on the northwest side of The Blob, just north of the Himmelhorn
routes.overview: A long, moderate-but-committing 16-pitch alpine rock line up the right edge of Ed Wood Memorial Buttress, finishing via the 'Bela Lugosi Traverse' along the ridge crest to the top of The Blob.
routes.beta: Fun, varied 5.9+ trad climbing over roughly 1,400 ft and 16 pitches up a prominent buttress in the Southern Pickets, just north of the Himmelhorn on the left (northwest) side of The Blob. The line climbs a steep dihedral partway up before joining the ridge (the 'Bela Lugosi Traverse') to reach The Blob's summit; exact difficulty and pitch count vary somewhat with the precise line chosen through the lower buttress.
routes.approach: Reached via the standard Southern Pickets approach: from the Goodell Creek trailhead/Upper Goodell Creek Group Camp area off SR 20 near Newhalem, follow the unmaintained climbers' path paralleling Goodell/Terror Creek for roughly 4-4.5 miles to a cairned junction, then climb steep, brushy, sometimes class-3 terrain up heather benches to a base camp in Terror Basin (about 6,000 ft of gain from the road; commonly a 7-8+ hour day to establish camp). From Terror Basin, parties cross toward the Himmelhorn/Crescent Creek Basin side of the divide to reach the buttress, found just north of the Himmelhorn on the left side of The Blob — look for a large gully with a prominent buttress on its right.
routes.descent: Traverse The Blob's summit ridge north to the Blob-Terror col, then rappel from webbing anchors and scramble down.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route on The Blob's summit ridge, traverse the ridge north to the Blob/Terror col. From the col, rappel using existing webbing anchors (carry extra tat/webbing, as there is no fixed hardware), then scramble down the remaining terrain, passing tarns/pools on the way back toward the Terror Basin/Crescent Creek side.
routes.bail: Because this is a long ridge-style route with no fixed rappel stations on the buttress itself, retreat from the climb is best done by rappelling the line of ascent off gear anchors built from the rack; the only established rappel anchors (webbing) are found at the Blob/Terror col reached after topping out, not partway up the route.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's length (16 pitches), its remote position, and the lack of fixed protection or rappel stations on the buttress itself, parties should set a firm turnaround based on daylight, weather, and the multi-hour approach/descent required to regain camp — North Cascades alpine weather can deteriorate quickly with no fast escape from the ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Typical for the Southern Pickets: mid-summer through early fall, roughly July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the approach benches and the buttress itself. Recorded ascents and trip reports in this part of the range cluster in late July through August.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this remote interior part of North Cascades National Park; a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack up to 3 inches — a single set of cams with doubles in common crack sizes, plus a set of nuts. No fixed anchors exist anywhere on the route, so carry extra slings/webbing for building gear belays and for backing up or replacing the rappel anchors at the Blob-Terror col.
routes.proNeeds: Gear to 3 inches; no fixed protection or bolted anchors anywhere on the route — every belay and the descent rappels are built from carried gear and webbing.
gear: Single rack of cams to 3 inches (doubled in common sizes)
whatToBring: Multi-day bivy/camping gear for the Terror Basin approach camp
Bear canister (required in many North Cascades NP backcountry zones)
Sturdy approach shoes for brushy, class-3 terrain
Extra webbing for anchor building/backup
Satellite communicator (no cell coverage)
NPS wilderness/backcountry permit
proTips: Difficulty and pitch count vary somewhat depending on exactly which line is taken through the lower buttress, per multiple route reports.
Camp in Terror Basin and cross toward the Himmelhorn/Crescent Creek side to shorten the push to the buttress's base.
watchOut: No fixed anchors on route — be ready to build every belay and rappel anchor from your own gear and carried webbing.
Remote, committing position with a long, brushy, multi-hour approach; retreat is slow.
knownHazards: Loose rock typical of Pickets alpine terrain.
Rapid weather changes common to North Cascades alpine ridges.
No fixed rappel or belay hardware anywhere on the route.

blurb: Lincoln Peak (9,085 ft), also called West Butte, is the westernmost and most technically difficult of the three Black Buttes, eroded remnants of an older stratovolcano perched on the southwest shoulder of Mount Baker in Washington's North Cascades. Built of loose, unstable volcanic rock mantled in permanent snow and ice, it presents a 1,000-ft north face and a 1,500-ft east face, and even its easiest line is considered one of the hardest standard routes in the Cascades. It was first climbed by Fred Beckey, Wesley Grande, John Rupley, and Herb Staley on July 22, 1956.

routes.overview: Lincoln Peak's only reasonably feasible line to the summit: a serpentine series of steep snow and ice chutes and faces on the southwest side of the peak, broken by a guarding serac and bergschrund, that only comes into safe condition for a few weeks each spring.
routes.beta: Known as the X Couloir or 'Emancipation Proclamation,' this is the standard (and only remotely feasible) route on Lincoln Peak, first climbed by Fred Beckey's party in 1956. It is less a single couloir than a connected series of steep snow/ice chutes and faces up the SW side of the peak, guarded by a bergschrund/serac crux around 7,400-7,600 ft, followed by narrow gullies, a knife-edge snow arete, and a final short scramble past crumbling rime-ice features to the summit. Only about a dozen-plus ascents are documented since 1956; it is climbable only in a narrow spring weather window before the route deteriorates into loose rock and open crevasses.
routes.approach: Historically reached via Forest Service Road 38 (Middle Fork Nooksack Road), gated in a normal year near milepost 9.9; parties drove to around the 2nd-3rd switchback (~2,650-2,780 ft) and continued by mountain bike or on foot along the decommissioned road toward the 6th-7th switchback (~3,700-3,740 ft). From there the route bushwhacks west across the shoulder of point 4481 through slide alder toward Rankin Creek, traverses into the Rankin Creek/Rankin Lake basin, and establishes high camp near treeline (roughly 5,100-6,100 ft). From camp, traverse below Lincoln Peak's southwest buttress (~6,200 ft) to gain the base of the X Couloir.
routes.descent: Reverse the X Couloir: a mix of downclimbing and rappelling, roughly 10 rappels when the route is icy.
routes.descentText: Parties generally descend the same line they climbed. One documented descent used 10 double-rope rappels: 4 off pickets placed on the way down, one off a sling placed on rock, and the remaining 5 off existing slings around rock horns left by prior parties. In soft spring snow the route has also been descended largely by downclimbing/glissading, and has even been skied top to bottom in favorable coverage.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: Day 1 - drive/bike/hike in to a high camp near the Rankin Creek basin/treeline (~6,000-6,100 ft); Day 2 - alpine start, climb the X Couloir to the summit and descend the same day, returning to camp; Day 3 - hike/bike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is down the same couloir. Below the crux serac/bergschrund, parties can generally downclimb or build rappel anchors off pickets or rock horns to reverse the route back to the Rankin Creek basin; above the crux, retreat is more committing.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time based on overnight freeze quality - this route depends on the snow, ice, and rock staying frozen together, and conditions deteriorate quickly once the sun is on the face, sharply increasing rockfall and wet-slide hazard on both the ascent and the descent.
routes.bestSeason: Late April through June is the accepted window, when cold overnight temperatures still lock the snow, ice, and loose rock together. Climb too early and the route may be under-formed or avalanche-prone; climb after the window closes (typically by mid-summer) and the bergschrund opens fully, ice cover thins, and rockfall/wet-slide hazard rises sharply.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Wesley Grande, John Rupley, and Herb Staley, July 22, 1956
routes.rappels: ~10 double-rope rappels in icy conditions (4 off placed pickets, 1 off a rock sling, 5 off existing slings around rock horns)
routes.detailedRack: Two ice tools per climber; 5-6 pickets; a small rack of nuts and 3-4 small cams for rock-horn anchors; a few ice screws for firm ice sections; two 60m ropes (a half rope plus a tag line has also been used) for the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets are the primary protection through the snow/ice gullies and bergschrund; carry a small nut/cam rack for building anchors off rock horns on the descent, plus a couple of ice screws for firmer ice pitches.
whatToBring: snowshoes or skis for the approach
overnight camping gear for a high camp near Rankin Creek/treeline
bivy or tent
sun protection for glacier/snow travel
satellite communicator (no reliable cell coverage)
proTips: Climb during a hard, cold overnight freeze (typically late April-early June) so the snow, ice, and loose rock are locked together for both the ascent and descent.
An early alpine start (pre-dawn) lets you tag the summit and start down before the upper gullies and the serac crux soften in the sun.
Scout rock horns on the way up - they're commonly used as rappel anchors on the way down.
watchOut: A guarding serac and bergschrund around 7,400-7,600 ft require direct 70-85 degree snow/ice climbing to pass.
The route's rock is loose, chossy volcanic material typical of the Black Buttes; rockfall increases sharply once the sun hits the gullies.
Crumbling, corniced rime-ice features guard the final short scramble to the summit.
knownHazards: Bergschrund crossing over a snow bridge below a roughly 50-ft serac.
Rockfall and wet-slide risk rise sharply once the route goes out of overnight-freeze condition.
A narrow, exposed snow-arete traverse partway up the route.

pitchDetail.notes: From the SW buttress base, ascend snow slopes toward the first bergschrund.
Cross a snow bridge and climb directly up the face of a ~50-ft serac.
Two roped 60m pitches through narrow gullies with runnel crossings.
Knife-edge arete requiring careful tool and crampon placement.
Final ~20 ft of scrambling on loose rock past crumbling rime-ice features to the summit.

routes.overview: Wilkes-Booth is a rarely-repeated ice line up Lincoln Peak's steep Northwest Face, established in a single 18.5-hour car-to-car push from the Heliotrope Ridge trailhead in March 2015 - only the second documented route on the peak.
routes.beta: First climbed by Daniel Coltrane and Michal Rynkiewicz on March 13, 2015, this Grade IV WI4 route takes roughly 2,000 vertical feet of ice up Lincoln Peak's Northwest Face via a crux 60m pitch of WI4 followed by about four more roped pitches on steepening ice and snowfields up to 70 degrees, finishing on a rime-covered ridge and an icy summit chimney. It remains one of only two known lines on the peak (alongside the SW-Face X Couloir standard route) and has seen very few repeats.
routes.approach: Approached in a single push from the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (Mount Baker's standard Coleman-Deming approach), crossing onto Mount Baker's upper glacier terrain to reach the base of Lincoln Peak's Northwest Face; the first-ascent party's full round trip took 18.5 hours.
routes.descent: The first-ascent party descended via Lincoln Peak's standard Southwest Face (X Couloir) route rather than reversing the climb.
routes.descentText: Rather than rappel the Northwest Face, the first-ascent party downclimbed the SW Face standard route, including sections of snow and ice up to 65 degrees, back toward the Heliotrope Ridge side.
routes.itinerary: The only documented ascent was a single 18.5-hour car-to-car push; given the objective hazard of glacier travel in the dark, most parties would instead plan an overnight approach or a very early alpine start.
routes.bail: No established bail points are documented; retreat from this steep, rarely-climbed ice face would be difficult above the lower pitches, so parties should treat it as a committing line once established on the route.
routes.turnaround: Given the lack of a proven descent line off this face, set a conservative turnaround time and be prepared to descend via the separate SW Face route rather than reversing the climb.
routes.bestSeason: Only one ascent is documented, climbed in mid-March; the route's WI4 ice likely requires sustained cold winter/early-spring conditions to form and would be expected to deteriorate by summer.
routes.fa: Daniel Coltrane and Michal Rynkiewicz, March 13, 2015
routes.season: winter-early spring (narrow ice window; only documented ascent was mid-March)
proTips: This route has essentially no repeat/beta history and no established descent line off the face itself - be prepared to build your own protection and to descend via the separate SW Face route instead of rappelling back down the line.
watchOut: The route sees very few ascents and has no established descent - the first-ascent party downclimbed the standard SW Face route instead of rappelling this line.
Rock quality is poor volcanic choss typical of the Black Buttes.
A rime-covered ridge and an icy chimney guard the summit just below the top.
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard under the winter/early-spring conditions this route has been climbed in.
Loose rock throughout the face.
A rimed, icy chimney just below the summit.

pitchDetail.notes: 60m of water ice, the technical crux.
Roughly four additional roped pitches on steepening ice and snowfields.
Rime-covered ridge and an icy chimney lead to the summit.

routes.corrections: No route named 'North Ridge' is documented on Lincoln Peak in any source checked (Peakbagger, SummitPost, Mountain Project, AAC Publications, Cascade Climbers, Lemke Climbs). 'North Ridge' is instead the name of a separate, well-known route on Mount Baker itself (a different, much larger peak nearby) - almost certainly the source of this mislabel. The only other documented route on Lincoln Peak besides the SW-Face 'X Couloir' standard route is a 2015 first-ascent ice line on the Northwest Face named 'Wilkes-Booth' (Grade IV WI4, 2,000'). Renamed this route entry and filled its fields using that route's real data; recommend verifying this routeId/routeName against its intended source before treating it as confirmed.

blurb: The Triad is a rugged, three-summited peak in the North Cascades National Park Complex, sitting on the southwest ridge extension of Eldorado Peak about 12 miles east of Marblemount, WA, in the Cascade River drainage. Built of North Cascades gneiss/orthogneiss and carrying a small hanging glacier on its south flank, it offers class 3-4 scrambling from the east side and harder climbing from the west-middle notch. It was first climbed in 1949 by Dick Eilertsen, Dick Lowery, Dick Scales, and Don Wilde, who jokingly called it "Three Dicks" for their shared first name before the fitting name "Triad" stuck.
hierarchyNote: None needed — verified real peak. Wikipedia (48°31'11"N 121°09'47"W), Natural Atlas (48.5196482558094, -121.162903582653), and PeakVisor (48.519499, -121.163023) all place The Triad within a few meters of the given coordinates, and all three cite 7,520+ ft, matching the given elevationFt exactly. It sits inside North Cascades National Park near Eldorado Peak/Cascade River, consistent with a "North Cascades Core" parent bucket.

routes.overview: The East Ridge / East Peak line is The Triad's standard route: a cross-country approach from the Hidden Lake Trail over a heather ridge, a descent and re-ascent of a loose gully to reach a small pocket glacier, and a short, exposed low-5th-class pitch that gains the summit ridge before an easy scramble to the top.
routes.beta: From the Hidden Lake Trailhead, follow the maintained trail up through forest and meadow, then leave it to traverse cross-country toward The Triad's east side. The route drops into and climbs back out of a steep, loose drainage before crossing a small glacier on the peak's south side to reach a high saddle. From the saddle, a wide ledge system leads toward the east ridge; gaining the ridge from this ledge is the technical crux — a single pitch of stiff 4th to low-5th class climbing (accounts vary by party and conditions) — after which class 3-4 scrambling leads to the summit. Most parties complete the route car-to-car in one long day.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road roughly 9.5-10.2 miles east from Marblemount, then turn onto Forest Service Road 1540 (signed for Hidden Lake Trail / Sibley Creek Road) and continue about 4.5-5 miles of rough, washout-prone gravel to its end at the Hidden Lake Trailhead (~3,600 ft; high-clearance/4WD recommended). Follow Hidden Lake Trail #745 uphill through forest and into wildflower meadows and heather; roughly an hour's hiking from the trailhead, leave the maintained trail and traverse cross-country east and up onto a ridge leading toward The Triad. Follow this ridge, then descend into a steep, loose gully/drainage (nicknamed the 'Drainage of Despair' in one trip report) and climb back out the other side to reach a small pocket glacier on the peak's south flank. Cross the glacier to a high saddle beneath the east ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb or rappel the crux pitch (commonly two ~30m rappels), re-cross the pocket glacier, then climb back through the loose drainage and over the approach ridge to regain the Hidden Lake Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 3-4 terrain back to the top of the crux pitch. Most parties rappel this section — commonly two rappels of roughly 30m off anchors/stations, so a rope with a tagline (or one longer single rope) is useful — though some downclimb it instead. Re-cross the pocket glacier (crampons/axe still useful here, especially once the snow softens in the afternoon), then re-descend into and climb back out of the loose drainage crossed on the approach. Regain the approach ridge and follow it back down to rejoin the Hidden Lake Trail for the hike out. Bear activity has been reported on the lower trail during the hike out.
routes.itinerary: Single long day, car-to-car: a pre-dawn start from the trailhead, roughly 6 hours to the summit via the trail, ridge, drainage, glacier, and crux pitch, then a similar or somewhat longer descent — reported round trips run approximately 10-11 hours total. Some parties instead camp near the base and split the climb over two days to reduce single-day exposure.
routes.bail: The technical crux has rappel anchors/stations allowing retreat back down to the glacier and ledge system below it. Below the crux the route is downclimbable scrambling; the main constraint on bailing is re-crossing the pocket glacier and the loose drainage, both easier and safer in cooler morning conditions than in afternoon heat.
routes.turnaround: Because the return crosses a glacier that softens through the day and a loose, unstable gully, parties should set a firm, conditions-based turnaround time rather than pushing for the crux late in the day — retreating with daylight and firmer snow/rock conditions in hand is safer than continuing into the afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed roughly July through September, with documented ascents into early-to-mid October in good years. Early-season snow on the approach ridge and glacier can ease travel but increases avalanche/wet-slab hazard on slopes below the west-middle notch; by mid-to-late summer the glacier is more likely to show bare ice, which can call for crampons and a tool even when the rock itself is dry.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected in this remote, glaciated terrain — NPS notes coverage is unreliable/fickle throughout the park backcountry. If a signal is found, calling North Cascades NP Dispatch at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37 is preferred over 911, which routes through the county and takes longer to reach park rescue resources.
routes.rappels: Commonly 2 rappels of roughly 30m off anchors near the crux (rope + tagline useful); some parties downclimb instead
objHaz: Bare ice on the glacier late-season
Wet-slab avalanche hazard below the west-middle notch in spring
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices for the route's single technical pitch: a small set of stoppers, a handful of small-to-mid cams, a couple of slings, and one 30m+ rope shared among the party. Full glacier-travel gear (harness, crampons, axe or light tool, and a means of crevasse rescue) is needed for the pocket glacier crossing on the south side.
routes.proNeeds: Only one short pitch of low-5th-class climbing needs protection; a light rack of small-to-mid cams and stoppers is adequate. The rest of the route is unroped scrambling and glacier travel.
gear: Ice axe or light ice tool
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
Gaiters
Sun protection (sunscreen, glacier glasses)
Extra water — long, exposed approach
Bear awareness (noise discipline) on the approach trail
Headlamp for an early alpine start
proTips: The crux ledge/pitch is protectable but sparse — a few small-to-mid cams and stoppers cover it rather than a full rack.
Aluminum crampons and a light axe/tool are typically sufficient for the pocket glacier in normal summer conditions.
Some parties downclimb the technical pitch unroped; roping up for the single exposed move is still common practice.
Start early — reported car-to-car times run roughly 10-11 hours even for efficient parties.
watchOut: Bear activity has been reported along the Hidden Lake approach trail.
The gully descent/re-ascent (the 'Drainage of Despair') is loose, dirty, and slow going.
Wet-slab avalanches have been observed releasing from slopes below the west-middle notch in spring — avoid that terrain when snow is warming.
knownHazards: Loose rock and unstable talus on the approach ridge and gully sections.
Crevasse hazard on the small glacier traversed on the peak's south side.
Bare, exposed ice on the glacier in late season can require crampons and an ice tool.
Avalanche and wet-slab hazard on snow slopes below the west-middle notch, especially in spring.

pitchDetail.notes: Heather ridge from the Hidden Lake Trail area, then a descent into and re-ascent out of a steep, loose drainage.
Cross a small pocket glacier on the peak's south side to reach the high saddle below the east ridge; bare ice possible.

pitchDetail.grade: Low 5th class (stiff 4th class per some parties)
pitchDetail.notes: Roped pitch gaining the east ridge from a ledge system; short, exposed, but with good holds throughout.
Final scrambling along the east ridge to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road + Forest Service Road 1540 (Sibley Creek Road)
road.seasonalGate: FS Road 1540 is typically impassable in winter/spring due to snow and washout damage; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF conditions before driving
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, cross the Skagit River and drive Cascade River Road roughly 9.5-10.2 miles, then turn onto FS Road 1540 (signed Hidden Lake Trail) and continue about 4.5-5 miles to its end at the trailhead; high-clearance/4WD strongly recommended.

climate.typical: Cool, wet winters with a heavy snowpack; a short, drier alpine climbing window from roughly July to September
climate.spring: Deep snowpack often persists into June/July, with wet-slab avalanche hazard reported on slopes below the west-middle notch
climate.summer: The main climbing window; the pocket glacier can show bare ice by mid-to-late summer and softens in afternoon sun
climate.fall: Climbable into September and sometimes October in dry years, with shorter days and possible early snow
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain make this a ski-mountaineering objective rather than a rock climb; approach road generally closed

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS) for the peak; Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest for the approach road and lower Hidden Lake Trail
access.fees: No North Cascades NP entrance fee; overnight backcountry permits cost $10/person plus a $6 transaction fee
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit from the NPS Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount is required for overnight stays; day climbs do not require a permit
access.passRequired: Sources conflict on whether a Northwest Forest Pass is needed at the Hidden Lake trailhead (it sits in Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF just outside the park boundary) — verify current trailhead signage before relying on this
access.closures: FS Road 1540 is seasonally closed/impassable in winter and spring due to snow and washouts

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit 911 — 911 for emergencies, (360) 428-3211 non-emergency; Skagit County Sheriff's Office coordinates backcountry Search and Rescue
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA
emergency.notes: For backcountry emergencies within the park, NPS recommends calling Park Dispatch at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37 if a signal is available, since routing through 911/county dispatch takes longer to reach park rescue resources. State your location and callback number immediately in case the call drops.

routes.corrections: The peak's overall 1949 first ascent (Eilertsen, Lowery, Scales, Wilde) is well documented, but no source specifies which of the three summits (east/middle/west) or which line that party climbed, so it is NOT attributed here as this route's FA. Sources also disagree slightly on the crux's difficulty — Wikipedia/older accounts call it 'stiff 4th class' while a 2022 trip report rates the same pitch 'low 5th class'; both characterizations are preserved above since it likely varies by conditions and party judgment.
